text: An explainable misinformation-verification system with retrieval, reasoning, analyst workflow, and report export in one platform.

text: Manual verification is slow and fragmented.
Ordinary tools do not connect verdicts, evidence, and workflow.
Classify, retrieve, reason, explain, and operationalize.
Reports, team review, settings, and analyst handoff.
Why misinformation verification is difficult in practice
Claims spread quickly across text, screenshots, and short videos, but reliable verification still depends on slow manual evidence collection and interpretation.
False or misleading content spreads in minutes, while manual cross-checking often takes much longer.
Sources, screenshots, and notes get scattered across tabs, chats, and ad hoc documents.
A simple label is not enough when an analyst must justify why a claim was supported or refuted.
We need a system that reduces verification time while preserving evidence quality, reasoning transparency, and an auditable review workflow.
The project is motivated by workflow failure, not only model accuracy.
What is missing in common verification setups
The gap is not just weak classification. The deeper gap is the absence of a unified pipeline from intake to evidence to final reporting.
Evidence gathering is slow and inconsistent.
Debate verdict plus trace and evidence tabs.
This slide turns the project into a clear design response to an identified gap.
Proposed solution: an end-to-end verification workspace
FactShield combines machine learning, retrieval, reasoning, and analyst operations so verification can move from raw claim to final report inside one system.
Accept claim text, image OCR output, or extracted video transcript.
Classify veracity and toxicity, then trigger search and retrieval when needed.
Use a structured debate to synthesize the final verdict.
Surface evidence, traces, and report-ready notes.
Reduce analyst effort without hiding the reasoning path.
Connect verification outputs to persistent reports and review workflows.
Present results through a modern SaaS interface rather than disconnected scripts.
This is the high-level answer to the gap slide.
The system is organized as a frontend workspace, a FastAPI backend, and a verification pipeline with persistence and retrieval services.
Command center, reports, team, settings, archive, and review surfaces.
Paste claims, open reports, assign investigations, export briefings.
Receives verification requests, manages task state, exposes feed and workspace APIs, and returns structured results.
Classification, search, FAISS retrieval, reranking, debate reasoning, and explainability.
SQLite tasks, workspace state, result export, cache, and rate limits.
Trusted article sources and retrieved candidate evidence.
Verdict, evidence, reports, team assignment, and settings-aware workflow.
This slide is your static architecture diagram for the viva-style part of the presentation.
This is the logic path that turns an incoming claim into an explainable verdict.
User submits text, image, or video-derived text.
XLM-R predicts veracity and toxicity priors.
Search plus FAISS fetch evidence candidates.
Prosecutor, defense, and judge synthesize verdict.
Evidence tab, trace, notes, and report summary.
When confidence is weak or uncertain, retrieval becomes more important.
Results become saved investigations and backend-backed report records.
The pipeline is designed so that model output is not the final artifact. The final artifact is an evidence-backed, reviewable verification result.
This is the clearest pipeline explanation slide.
How a user moves through FactShield
This operator workflow shows how the product turns backend intelligence into an actual review process.
Paste claim or upload media, then start analysis.
Immediate system response: status, pipeline stage, retrieved evidence.

text: Save metadata, assign investigators, update workspace controls, and export the final report.
Decision-makers receive a downloadable report with verdict, confidence, notes, and evidence summary.
Use this slide to connect technical depth to actual product use.
We redesigned the frontend to behave like a coherent operator product rather than a disconnected set of screens.
A strong product slide matters because it proves implementation maturity.
The project now includes backend-backed workflow features, not only verification endpoints.
Export produces a real downloadable report instead of a visual placeholder button.
Reports are now derived from persisted verification tasks and live backend records.
Investigations can be assigned through the Team workspace and persisted.
Workspace controls save status, detail, and value instead of staying static.
Reports store owner, audience, approval status, reviewer identity, notes, and export metadata.
Historic stored payloads were cleaned so report-facing text remains presentation-ready.
This is the implementation-summary slide for the product engineering part.
We tested a short set of claims against the live backend to identify strong demo paths and risky edge cases.

text: These results are why tomorrow's demo should stay inside rehearsal-tested claims.
A credible system presentation should acknowledge what is working now and what still deserves improvement.

1. Vague claims can still receive strong verdicts.
2. The strongest demo path is still text-first.
3. Wider multimodal validation is still limited.
4. Some judgments still depend on retrieval quality.

1. Better uncertainty handling for weak inputs.
2. More image and video demo validation.
3. Richer reviewer audit trail.
4. Expanded evidence-source diversity.

text: The system already connects ML, retrieval, explainability, persistence, and product workflow in one coherent implementation.
Good presentation framing: this project solves the integrated verification workflow problem and creates a solid base for deeper multimodal and uncertainty-aware research.
Faculty usually appreciate a strong limitations slide.
FactShield is an explainable misinformation-verification platform that connects technical analysis to real analyst workflow.
Slow, fragmented, and weakly explainable verification workflows.
A pipeline that classifies, retrieves, reasons, explains, and operationalizes results.
A SaaS interface with reports, assignments, settings, and export built on live backend data.
Thank you. The next step in the presentation is the live walkthrough using the tested claim sequence.
End here before switching to the live product demo.
